coredata.dc:title: Bifunctional S, N-Codoped carbon dots-based novel electrochemiluminescent bioassay for ultrasensitive detection of atrazine using activated mesoporous biocarbon as enzyme nanocarriers
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Yunqing
Chen, Yuxin
Zhang, Shiqin
Zhang, Lizhi
Gong, Jingming
coredata.dc:description: A novel and ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescent (ECL) bioassay has been developed for the detection of atrazine (ATZ), whereby bifunctional S, N-codoped carbon dots (S, N-CDs) and activated mesoporous bicarbons (BCs) have been innovatively integrated to synergistically amplify the ECL signal. When endogenous dissolved O2 is used as a coreactant, its sluggish reduction hinders the enhancement of ECL intensity of the luminophore, thus restricting its further application in bioanalysis. Here, bifunctional S, N-CDs severe as not only the ECL luminophore but the coreaction accelerator of dissolved O2 to generate more intermediates to boost the coreaction without using any other coreactant and coreaction accelerator. The as-formed nanoarchitectures of BCs possess enlarged surface area as the nanocarriers. They could provide adequate active sites for immobilization of tyrosinase (Tyr), which greatly prompts the ECL bioassay applications. Such a smart integration of bifunctional S, N-CDs, activated mesoporous BCs and the enzyme inhibition reaction achieves a unique and attractive high-performance signal-on ECL bioassay, realizing ultrasensitive detection of ATZ. Under the optimal condition, this bioassay exhibits two linear ranges, from 0.0001 to 0.01 μg L−1 and 0.01–20 μg L−1 with a detection limit of 0.08 ng L−1 at signal to noise ratio of 3. The as-fabricated assay is sensitive and economical, opening a new way for the enhancement of ECL signal output and a versatile strategy for ultrasensitive ECL bioanalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bifunctional S
N-codoped carbon dots
Mesoporous bicarbons
Electrochemiluminescent bioassay
Dissolved oxygen
Atrazine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of S, N co-doped carbon dots
Characterization of S, N-CDs and biocarbon
ECL behaviors of the as-prepared bioassay
Analytical performance of S, N-CDs-based ECL bioassay
Selectivity of the ATZ ECL bioassay

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Photoluminescence and UV–vis absorption spectra, (B) XRD pattern, (C) FTIR spectrum and (D) XPS spectrum of the as-prepared S, N-CDs. Inset: Photograph of the S, N-CDs solution under irradiation of natural light (left) and UV light (right).

SEM images of (A) the raw air-dried bananal peel powder, (B) the resultant activated BCs; (C) XRD pattern of raw air-dried bananal peel (a), and the resultant BCs (b); and (D) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms, before (a) and after (b) carbonization. Inset: BJH pore size distribution of the resultant activated BCs.

(A) EIS plot of (a) bare Au electrode, (b) BCs/Au, and (c) Tyr-BCs/Au in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 0.01 M [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-. The applied frequency ranging from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz; (B) ECL-potential curves of bare Au electrode (a), BCs/Au (b), Tyr/Au(c), Tyr-BCs/Au (d), Tyr-BCs/Au with 100 mM phenol in air-saturated 0.1 M pH 6.5 PBS (e), Tyr-BCs/Au in air-saturated 0.1 M pH 6.5 PBS containing S, N-CDs (f), and Tyr-BCs/Au air-saturated 0.1 M pH 6.5 PBS containing 100 mM phenol + S, N-CDs (g); (C) ECL-potential curves of S, N-CDs at Tyr-BCs/Au in N2-saturated (a), air-saturated (b), 320 mM H2O2 + N2-saturated (c), O2-saturated(d) and air-saturated containing 20 μL isopropanol (e), 0.2 mg mL−1 SOD (f), and 1 mM l-cysteine (g) in 0.1 M pH 6.5 PBS; Inset: the corresponding CV curves of S, N-CDs in N2-saturated (a), and air-saturated (b) in 0.1 M PBS; (D) ECL responses of S, N-CDs at Tyr-BCs/Au in air-saturated 0.1 M pH 6.5 PBS.

Effects of (A) solution pH, (B) potential scan rate, the amount of (C) S, N-CDs, (D) BCs dispersed onto Au electrode, (E) the loaded Tyr (3 mg mL−1) and (F) immersion time of the as-prepared sensor in ATZ (5 μg L−1) solution on ECL peak intensity of S, N-CDs at Tyr-BCs/Au.

(A) Effect of the concentration of ATZ on the ECL intensity of S, N-CDs at Tyr-BCs/Au (from a to m, 0, 0.0001, 0.0003, 0.0006, 0.001, 0.008, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, and 20 μg L−1) in 100 mM phenol + air-saturated 0.1 M pH 6.5 PBS solution; Inset: the calibration curve of the ECL intensity against the concentration of ATZ; (B) ECL intensity of S, N-CDs in the presence of 50.0 μg L−1 each compound with and without 5.0 µg L−1 ATZ.
Schematic illustration of the principle for ECL bioassay for the detection of ATZ.
ECL determination of ATZ in different water samples using the proposed method (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Bifunctional S, N-Codoped carbon dots-based novel electrochemiluminescent bioassay for ultrasensitive detection of atrazine using activated mesoporous biocarbon as enzyme nanocarriers

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Pesticide & Chemical Biology of Ministry of Education, International Joint Research Center for Intelligent Biosensing Technology and Health, College of Chemistry, Central China Normal University, Wuhan, 430079, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Pesticide & Chemical Biology of Ministry of Education
International Joint Research Center for Intelligent Biosensing Technology and Health

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Pesticide & Chemical Biology of Ministry of Education, International Joint Research Center for Intelligent Biosensing Technology and Health, College of Chemistry, Central China Normal University, Wuhan, 430079, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel and ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescent (ECL) bioassay has been developed for the detection of atrazine (ATZ), whereby bifunctional S, N-codoped carbon dots (S, N-CDs) and activated mesoporous bicarbons (BCs) have been innovatively integrated to synergistically amplify the ECL signal. When endogenous dissolved O2 is used as a coreactant, its sluggish reduction hinders the enhancement of ECL intensity of the luminophore, thus restricting its further application in bioanalysis. Here, bifunctional S, N-CDs severe as not only the ECL luminophore but the coreaction accelerator of dissolved O2 to generate more intermediates to boost the coreaction without using any other coreactant and coreaction accelerator. The as-formed nanoarchitectures of BCs possess enlarged surface area as the nanocarriers. They could provide adequate active sites for immobilization of tyrosinase (Tyr), which greatly prompts the ECL bioassay applications. Such a smart integration of bifunctional S, N-CDs, activated mesoporous BCs and the enzyme inhibition reaction achieves a unique and attractive high-performance signal-on ECL bioassay, realizing ultrasensitive detection of ATZ. Under the optimal condition, this bioassay exhibits two linear ranges, from 0.0001 to 0.01 μg L−1 and 0.01–20 μg L−1 with a detection limit of 0.08 ng L−1 at signal to noise ratio of 3. The as-fabricated assay is sensitive and economical, opening a new way for the enhancement of ECL signal output and a versatile strategy for ultrasensitive ECL bioanalysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to increased concerns on food safety, health issues, and environmental protection, various analytical assays have exhibited flourishing developments [1–3]. In particular a sensitive, low-cost, robust, and on-site detection approach for rapid screening of trace amounts of analyte in different matrices (mainly waters, food, and soil) [4–6] is urgently needed to ensure public health and environmental management.

Electrochemiluminescence (ECL), as a promising analytical technique with versatility, high sensitivity, low-cost and simplified instruments [7–9], has attracted extensive attention and inaugurated an innovative field in environmental [10] and biological analysis [11,12]. Nowadays, much effort has been made to enhance the detection sensitivity in the development of ECL assay, making it more reliable and applicable. The individual ECL luminophore could not offer a strong ECL signal. In contrast, the binary ECL system composed of ECL luminophore and coreactant (e.g., S2O8 2−, H2O2, TPrA, and C2O4 2−) has become the most popular for greatly enhancing ECL signal [13–15]. Among them, one of the most typical ECL coreactants, peroxydisulfate ion (S2O8 2−), has been widely used by cooperation with various ECL luminophore, including ruthenium as well as its derivatives [16], graphite carbon nitride (C3N4) [17], ultrathin C3N4 nanosheets [18], nanoclusters [19], quantum dots (QDs) [20], and so on. To further meet the increasing demand for ultraselectively detecting residues in matrices, recently, the ternary ECL system has been proposed by introducing extra novel coreaction accelerator into the ECL system [21]. For example, as coreaction accelerator, semi-carbazide [22], aminobenzene [23] and hemin/G-wire supramolecular nanostructure [24] have been introduced into ECL systems, respectively, prompting the conversion of S2O8 2− into massive SO4 -, thus leading to an amplified ECL signal. More recently, Yuan et al. developed a novel ternary ECL system using the grafted Pt nanoparticles as efficient coreaction accelerator of dissolved O2 for the detection of MicroRNA [21]. Unlike the strongly oxidized coreactant of S2O8 2−, the endogenous coreactant of dissolved O2 possessing the mild reaction and easy operation properties, has been explored in various ECL systems [21,25,26]. However, the reaction activity of dissolved O2 is often sluggish, unbeneficial to achieve a significantly enhanced ECL signal, which hampered its applications on the ECL assay, especially for the detection of trace amounts of analyte in matrices. In this regard, the introduction of coreaction accelerator, i.e. the utilization of novel ternary ECL system indeed provides a promising approach for ultrasensitive ECL assay, but the intrinsic properties of coreaction accelerator, such as the scarcity, high cost, and low tolerance of Pt to poisoning might restrict the universal applicability of the ternary ECL system. Meanwhile, the extra addition of coreaction accelerator into ECL system might introduce potential interference and have contamination risk on electrode. Obviously, the elaborate design via smartly integrating the coreaction accelerator with ECL system is highly desirable.

Owing to the excellent photostability, favorable biocompatibility, low-cost, and high resistance to photobleaching, various carbon nanostructures-based ECL emitter, including carbon dots (CDs) [20], graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) [17], carbon nitride nanosheets [18], and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [27], etc have been developed and played great roles in the areas of biochemical analysis, clinical medicine, and environmental testing. Particularly, as emerging most remarkable families of materials, heteroatom-doped carbon nanostructures (e.g. boron, nitrogen, fluorine and sulfur, etc) have sparked excitement in their analytical and energy applications due to their outstanding optical and electrical properties with tunable surface state as well as local chemical features [25,28–31]. These fine-tuned heteroatom-doped GQDs-S2O8 2− coreactant ECL systems recently have been constructed to detect small molecules, organic compound and protein [28,32], respectively. Notably, heteroatom-doped carbon nanostructures have been explored to possess high activities in oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) for energy applications [33]. Inspired by this, we for the first time establish an attractive S, N-CDs-the dissolved O2 coreactant ECL bioassay, where S, N-CDs sever as both the ECL luminophore and the coreaction accelerator of dissolve O2, i.e. bifunctional S, N-codoped CDs-based novel ECL system. This as-established novel ECL system is expected to amplify the ECL signal significantly without introducing any strong oxidative coreactant or extra coreaction accelerator. Furthermore, the capability of the present ECL system for detecting Atrazine (ATZ used as the model, a typical one of the most broadly used pesticides associated with the ecotoxicological effects) [34–36] has been explored.

Thanks to the inhibition effect of ATZ towards the catalytic activity of the blue copper protein of tyrosinase (Tyr) [37], herein, the activated porous biocarbon nanoarchitectures have been innovatively used as the immobilization matrix of enzyme Tyr. Recently, porous carbon materials from agricultural biomass (e.g. leaves, arundo, coconut fiber, tobacco stems, bamboo, and crop residues) [38–40] have received widespread attention because of their unique properties, such as low cost, high specific surface area, large pore volume, and abundant surface oxygen functional groups, finding appealing applications on CO2 capture [38,41], adsorption of benzene volatile organic compounds adsorption [42,43] and hydrogen-evolution reaction, etc. [44]. Featured with the above unique properties, the porous biocarbons (BCs) exhibit great promising on enabling the immobilization of Tyr fluently. Under the catalytic effect of Tyr, the substrate phenol can be efficiently transformed into quinone in the presence of dissolved oxygen [45]. Upon the addition of ATZ, the inhibition on the activity of Tyr would occur, thereby inducing the consumed dissolved oxygen decrease in enzyme reaction, which in turn makes more dissolved oxygen involved in co-reactants of ECL, thus leading to the enhanced ECL signal (shown in Scheme 1 ). Meanwhile, bifunctional S, N-codoped CDs not only sever as luminophore but also as the correaction accelerator of dissolve O2, efficiently prompt the reduction of dissolved oxygen to produce more reactive oxygen species (ROS) to participate in ECL reaction with a remarkable enhancement of ECL intensity. Until now, a study using bifunctional S, N-CDs and activated mesoporous BCs as the immobilization matrix of Tyr to synergistically amplify the ECL signal has not yet been reported.

In this work, a novel bifunctional S, N-CDs-based ECL bioassay has been constructed for the ultrasensitive detection of ATZ, via smartly integrating enzyme-inhibition principle, activated mesoporous BCs as the immobilization matrix of Tyr and the bifunctional S, N-CDs for signal amplification. The proposed ECL bioassay displays a signal-on ECL response toward the increase of the concentration of ATZ, achieving ultrasensitive detection of ATZ with a detection limit as low as 0.08 ng L−1. Such the newly constructed bifunctional S, N-CDs-based ECL bioassay opened a new way for the enhancement of ECL signal and a versatile strategy for detection of trace analytes.

Tyrosinase (Tyr), bromoxynil (BMN), malathion (MLT) and profenofos (PFF) were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Co. (Shanghai, China). Atrazine (ATZ) was from TCI (Shanghai, China). l-cysteine was obtained from REGAL Biotech Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Triethanolamine, melamine, glucosamine, citric acid were obtained from SinoPharm (China). Other chemicals were used as received and of analytical grade. Phosphate buffer solutions (PBS, 0.1 M) with different pH values were prepared using stock standard solutions of K2HPO4 and KH2PO4. Ultrapure water with resistivity higher than 18 MΩ cm was used. All instruments used are listed in Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of S, N co-doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (S, N-CDs) were prepared according to the reported methods [46]. Briefly, 4.0 g of citric acid and 1.8 g of l-cysteine were placed in a flask, heated to 220 °C, and then kept stirring for 4 h. Subsequently, the obtained product was dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water, followed by adjusting the pH of the solution to 7.0 with sodium hydroxide solution. The resulting S, N-CDs solution was collected into centrifuge tube and stored at 4 °C in the dark. For comparison, undoped CDs were also prepared for ECL measurements (detailed info listed in supporting information).

A simple carbonization approach, coupled with chemical activation by solid ZnCl2 was applied to synthesize the activated biocarbons (BCs) using abundant available biomass banana peel [38]. In a typical synthesis, the banana peel was cut into small pieces, washed three times with ultrapure water, and then bone-dried in an oven at 100 °C lasting for an extended period for complete removal of moisture. After that, the chemical activation was initiated by thoroughly mixing 1 g of the finely powdered biomass with 3 g of solid ZnCl2. Thereafter, the mixture was carbonized at 500 °C (heating rate: 10 °C min−1) for 2 h in a tube furnace with a flowing nitrogen. After carbonization, the carbon black powder was repeatedly washed with 1 M HCl and distilled water until the pH of the filtrate solution was near neutral. Finally, the obtained materials were dried overnight at 100 °C in a hot air oven (labeled as BCs).

Prior to modification, the basal Au electrode was polished using 1.0, 0.3 and 0.05 μm α-Al2O3 slurries to achieve a mirror finish. After each polishing, the electrode was sonicated in ethanol and doubly distilled water for 5 min, successively. Followed that, the electrode was electrochemically cleaned in 0.5 M H2SO4 by cycling potential between −0.3 and 1.5 V (versus SCE) until a stable gold oxidation/reduction CV obtained, rinsed with water, and dried with nitrogen gas ready for use. Afterwards, 10 μL of a certain concentration of biocarbon dispersion was dropped onto the surface of a clean gold electrode and kept at room temperature till dry (labeled as BCs/Au). BCs/Au was further coated with a certain amount of Tyr (3 mg mL−1), 5 μL of 0.5% Nafion solution, successively, and dried at 4 °C for further use (labeled as Tyr-BCs/Au).

For the detection of the target ATZ, the as-prepared Tyr-BCs/Au was first incubated in PBS solution containing a certain concentration of ATZ for a given time, and then rinsed with water. Then, it was transferred into the electrolyte (0.1 M, pH 6.5 PBS) containing 100 mM phenol (with 10 μL of a desired concentration of S, N-CDs dispersion added) for performing the ECL measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of S, N-CDs and biocarbon
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1A shows the UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of the S, N-CDs. The UV–vis spectrum shows two typical peaks, assigned to π-π* transition of C=C (centered at 240 nm) and n-π* transition of C=O (around 343 nm), respectively [29]. As known, the adsorption at 343 nm could result in strong emission due to the trapping of excited-state energy by the surface states. Expectedly, under the irradiation at 365 nm, the light yellow S, N-CDs dispersion solution emitted a strong blue fluorescence, centered at 425 nm (Fig. 1 A). The X-Ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern displays a broader peak located at ∼ 27° (Fig. 1B), corresponding to the (002) lattice space of the graphene, confirming the presence of graphene structure of the obtained S, N-CDs [29].

To characterize the surface functional groups of the S, N-CDs, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra are performed (shown in Fig. 1C). The adsorption peaks at ∼3432 and 2361 cm−1 can be ascribed to stretching vibrations of O-H/N-H and S-H, respectively [29,47]. Another two stretching bands at 1703 cm−1and 1603 cm−1 are associated with the νC=O in the COOH, and νC=C in the aromatic group [47], respectively. Meanwhile, the observed peaks at ∼1505 and 1397 cm−1 belong to bending vibration of C-H and stretching vibration of C-N, respectively [48]. In addition, peaks at ∼1227 and 2072 cm−1 are the stretching vibration of the C=S and –SCN group, respectively [47,49]. The above results demonstrated that functional groups such as –NH, –SH, oxygen-containing groups (–OH, –COOH, –C=O, C–O), C–N, –C=S, and –C=C exist on the surface of S, N-CDs.

The obtained X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results also confirm the successful doping of N and S atoms in the as-prepared S, N-CDs. The XPS survey scan of S, N-CDs shows five characteristic peaks at about 162.7, 227.8, 285.5, 399.4 and 531.0 eV, corresponding to S2p, S2s, C1s, N1s, and O1s, clearly revealing the presence of C, O, N and S elements in the prepared S, N-CDs (displayed in Fig. 1D). The high resolution spectrum of C1s, O1s, N1s and S2p further confirms that functional groups, such as –COOH,–OH, –NH2, and –SCN coexiste on the surface of S, N-CDs (Fig. S1), which agrees well with the FTIR results. These results have demonstrated that S, N-codoped carbon dots have been successfully synthesized.

The resultant biocarbon materials obtained from the parent biomass (banana peels) have also been characterized. Seen from the scanning electron microscope (SEM) image (Fig. 2 A), the raw air-dried banana peels exhibit a smooth sheet-like morphology with a few thin wrinkles. After carbonization, the resultant activated BCs display sponge-like nanoparticle aggregations (Fig. 2B).

Fig. 2C displays the typical XRD result of the resultant biocarbon materials. For the raw air-dried banana peels, only a weak and broad peak at ∼26.5 °C was observed, attributed to the amporous carbon (curve a). Whereas, two broad peaks centered at 23.7° and 43.4° match with the reflection of (002) and (101) crystal planes in a graphitic phase (curve b), respectively, suggesting the presence of graphitic layers in the resultant activated BCs [38]. Interestingly, the interlayer distance of the prepared carbon layer is much higher than that of graphite (2θ = 26.4°), a typical feature of porous activated carbon [38].

Fig. 2D presents the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of the as-prepared carbon materials. The BCs isotherm exhibits type IV isotherm with a H1 hysteresis loops (curve b), indicating the existence of mesoporous structure in BCs with the pore diameter ranging from 2 to 4 nm. While a type H3 hysteresis isotherm is observed for the raw banana peel with a typical descent in the desorption branch (curve a). Compared with that of the raw banana peel (∼76.7 m2/g), the BET surface area of biocarbon (∼1636.3 m2/g) was greatly enhanced. The pore diameter of the produced BCs ranges from about 2 to 4 nm (inset of Fig. 2D), further indicating the high specific surface area and mesoporous properties. Obviously, the as-formed BCs nanoarchitectures with enlarged surface area possess adequate active sites for the further immobilization of tyrosinase, thereby favoring the followed ECL bioassay applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL behaviors of the as-prepared bioassay
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the successful fabrication of the present ECL bioassay, the EIS measurements and ECL behaviors are investigated after each decoration step. The typical EIS of each electrode after each decoration steps are shown in Fig. 3 A. EIS of the bare Au electrode is composed of a semicircle and a straight line featuring a diffusion-limiting step of the Fe(CN)6 3−/4− (curve a). With the modification of BCs onto Au (BCs/Au), the semicircles increase, indicating a barrier effect from BCs film toward the interfacial charge transfer (curve b). The sequential immobilization of nonconductive tyrosinase onto BCs/Au (labeled as Tyr–BCs/Au) further hinders the electron transfer, resulting in an increase of the semicircles (curve c). It further confirms the fine feasibility of the activated BCs as enzyme nanocarriers.

To monitor the fabrication of the ECL bioassays, the corresponding ECL signals at each modification step are also observed (Fig. 3B). By cycling the potential between 0.00 and −1.80 V (vs Ag/AgCl), the ECL emission of bare Au electrode (curve a), BCs/Au (curve b), Tyr/Au (curve c), Tyr-BCs/Au (curve d) and Tyr-BCs/Au with phenol (curve e) are negligible, but upon addition of S, N-CDs, an intense ECL emission of Tyr-BCs/Au could be observed, around 4300 (curve f). With the addition of phenol (curve g), the Tyr catalyzed its oxidation to produce quinone using dissolved O2 as oxidant (also the coreactant of the present ECL system). Dissolved O2 participates in enzyme reaction, which weakens its involvement in ECL process with the ECL intensity decreased. Because no obvious ECL signals could be observed at bare Au electrode, BCs/Au, Tyr/Au, Tyr-BCs/Au and Tyr-BCs/Au with phenol, the ECL emission should be ascribed to S, N-QDs.

In the following experiments, effects of dissolved oxygen were also investigated on ECL intensity (shown in Fig. 3C). In air-saturated PBS solution, the ECL response of S, N-CDs at Tyr-BCs/Au is about 4300 at – 1.77 V (curve b). Upon removing dissolved O2 by bubbling N2 into the solution, the ECL peak intensity decreased dramatically (curve a), around 235, suggesting that as an important coreactant, dissolved O2 efficiently prompts the strong ECL signal. Meanwhile, the ECL peak of S, N-CDs doesn't disappear completely, indicating that dissolved O2 isn't completely eliminated from the nanocarriers field of Tyr-BCs/Au by bubbling the atmosphere of N2, i.e. a potential adsorption of O2. Compared with the synchronous CV curve at Tyr-BCs/Au in N2-saturated PBS containing S, N-CDs (curve a, inset), an obvious cathodic wave with onset and peak potential at − 0.82 and −1.23 V (vs. Ag/AgCl), respectively, is found in air-saturated PBS solution (curve b, inset), undoubtedly due to the reduction of oxygen. Interestingly, when replaced S, N-CDs with undoped CDs, the ECL signal of undoped CDs at Tyr-BCs/Au is negligibly weak whether in air/N2-saturated PBS (Fig. S2). This further intensifies the capability of heteroatom-doped carbon nanostructures for promoting the reduction of oxygen, agreeing well with that heteroatom-doped carbon nanostructures possess high activities in ORR [25,33,50]. Obviously, S, N-CDs not only sever as the ECL luminophore, but also greatly facilitate the reduction of oxygen, i.e. the coreaction accelerator of dissolve O2, no need the extra addition of coreactant and coreaction accelerator.

To further verify the ECL mechanism of the S, N-CDs, the discrepancy of ECL signals at Tyr-BCs/Au were measured under different conditions (Fig. 3C). One can see that the ECL intensity of S, N-CDs in deaerated PBS solution containing 320 mM H2O2 (approximates the saturated concentration of dissolved O2 at room temperature under standard atmospheric pressure), is roughly equal to that of saturated oxygen (curve c &d). And they are stronger than that of S, N-CDs in the air-saturated PBS, which indicates that both dissolved O2 and H2O2 can serve as the effective coreactant for the ECL system. Furthermore, with the introduction of the related radical scavengers into the air-saturated PBS solution, such as isopropanol (a hydroxyl radical scavenger, curve e), superoxide dismutase (SOD, a superoxide anion scavenger, curve f) and l-cysteine (scavenger of hydroxyl and superoxide radicals, curve g), the corresponding ECL responses also display an 29%, 58% and 85% decrease, respectively, implying that ROS (such as •OH, O2 •−) are indeed involved in the above ECL emission. So far, these clearly demonstrated that the bifunctional S, N-CDs could greatly facilitate the reduction of the dissolve O2 generating more ROS to react with S, N-CDs radical (S, N-CDs• −) to improve the ECL reaction efficiency (Eqs. (1)-6)). The possible mechanism of the bifunctional S, N-CDs-based novel ECL system has been listed in the following route:
(1) S, N-CDs + e− → S, N-CDs•−
(2) O2 + e−→ O2 •−
(3) O2+ 2e−+ 2H+ → H2O2
(4) O2 • −+ H2O2 + H+ → O2 + H2O + OH•
(5) S, N-CDs• −+OH• + H+ → S, N-CDs∗ + H2O
(6) S, N-CDs∗ →S, N-CDs + hv

To evaluate the stability and reproducibility of the present ECL system in air-saturated PBS solution containing S, N-CDs, the replicated cyclic voltammetric sweeps (n = 10) are performed (displayed in Fig. 3D). Strong and highly repeatable ECL signals are obtained. The proposed dual-function S, N- CDs-based novel ECL bioassay displays a stable and amplified ECL signal, further demonstrating its feasibility for ECL biosensing trace ATZ.

To get high-performance ECL response, the related experimental parameters are optimized. Seen from Fig. 4 A, the ECL emission of S, N-CDs is affected by pH of the solution. While pH ranging from 5.5 to 6.5, the ECL signal increases and then drops at high pH in the range of 6.5–7.5. At lower pH, proton in the solution is easily reduced at the negative potential, impeding the reduction of the coreactant oxygen. Considering the later real applications, pH 6.5 was used for this work.

Fig. 4B shows the effect of the scan rate on the ECL signal in a range of 50–400 mV/s. Since the formation rate of the excited state and the diffusion rate of the co-reactants directly influence the ECL efficiency, too low or high scan rate will affect the ECL intensity [51]. The resulting optimized scan rate is to be 200 mV/s.

As ECL luminophore and coreaction accelerator of dissolve O2, the amount of S, N-CD remarkably affects the ECL intensity (Fig. 4C). With the addition of S, N-CDs in the air-saturated PBS solution, the ECL intensity is first increased steadily up to 0.5 mg mL−1, and then decreases at higher amount. It is likely to attribute that the excessive addition of S, N-CDs may cause the aggregation of nanostructured CDs and even inhibit the electron exchange with ROS. Thus, the added volume of S, N-CDs is chosen to be 0.5 mg mL−1.
Herein, the as-formed activated mesoporous BCs nanoarchitecture is used as the nanocarriers of.

Tyr enzyme. The amount of BCs dispersed onto Au electrode is directly related with the followed ECL bioassay applications. Seen from Fig. 4D, the ECL signal reaches maximum with the amount of BC increased up to 0.06 mg mL−1 (10 μL), indicating that the nanocarriers of BCs could provide the sufficient active sites for the immobilization of Tyr.

Meanwhile, the loaded amount of Tyr enzyme is also one of the most influential parameters. Too lower or higher amount of Tyr would affect the efficiency of enzymatic reactions and even the followed ECL biosensing ATZ (Fig. 4E). The optimal amount of Tyr (3 mg mL−1) is 10 μL.

Fig. 4F shows the effect of the reaction time on the ECL intensity of S, N-CDs by immersing the Tyr-BCs/Au into ATZ solution. With prolonging the immersion time of Tyr-BCs/Au into ATZ solution (5 μg L−1), the ECL signal steadily increases till time up to 20 min and then drops. Initially, with increasing the reaction time, it could ensure that ATZ reacts adequately with Tyr. When exposed to ATZ solution for a long time, the structure and activity of Tyr may be weakened. Therefore, the optimal reaction time for the following ECL measurements is selected to be 20 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of S, N-CDs-based ECL bioassay
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimum experimental conditions, the bifunctional S, N-CDs-based ECL bioassay is applied for the determination of ATZ with an ECL signal increased successively after soaking in a solution containing various concentrations of ATZ (Fig. 5 A). The linear relationship between the obtained ECL intensity and the logarithm of ATZ concentrations could be achieved in two ranges, from 0.0001 to 0.01 μg L−1 and 0.01–20 μg L−1, respectively (inset of Fig. 5 A). The corresponding linear equations are: ECL intensity = 1102 + 5554 log C/μg L−1 (R = 0.994) and ECL intensity = 228 + 3930 log C/μg L−1 (R = 0.996), respectively. The detection limit is estimated to be 0.00008 μg L−1 (i.e. 0.08 ng L−1) based on the signal-to-noise ratio of 3, which is much lower than those previously reported values (shown in Table S1). The outstanding analytical performance could be attributed to the smart integration of the mesoporous BCs with high surface area as the nanocarriers of Tyr and the bifunctional S, N-CDs as both luminophore and the correaction accelerator for signal amplification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the ATZ ECL bioassay
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the selectivity of the sensor for ATZ detection, some inorganic ions (such as Cl−, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, etc), and other typical pesticides with similar structure or function, including profenofos (PFF), bromoxynil (BMN), malathion (MLT), and pentachlorophenol (PCP), are chosen as the interfering substances, respectively. It can be found that the ECL signal of each interfering compound alone (each with a concentration of 50.0 μg L−1) is very close to the background signal (Fig. 5B), while the ECL response is evidently increased upon the addition of ATZ (5.0 μg L−1). Moreover, in the bi-component system with ATZ (5.0 μg L−1) and individual interfering compound (each with 50.0 μg L−1), the ECL response is almost unchanged with the ECL intensity deviations all less than 3.6%, showing a high selectivity of the present ECL bioassay.

The feasibility of the present bioassay has been further evaluated by processing real water samples that were collected from Yangtze River (Wuhan, China) and East Lake (Wuhan, China). After the collected water samples were filtered by a nylon membrane (0.22 μm), the recovery experiments were performed by adding different amounts of ATZ into real samples. The recoveries of the spiked ATZ in real water samples range from 94.0% to 106% (listed in Table 1 ). These results demonstrate that the proposed strategy is quite effective for analyzing real samples, with high accuracy and reproducibility.

To summarize, a novel ECL bioassay has been successfully fabricated for the ultrasensitive detection of ATZ using the only endogenous coreactant of dissolved O2, coupling with the activated mesoporous BCs as the nanocarriers of Tyr and the bifunctional S, N-CDs as both luminophore as well as the correaction accelerator. The bifunctional S, N-CDs efficiently facilitate the reduction of the coreactant (dissolved O2), generating more ROS to react with S, N-CDs radicals, thus remarkably enhancing the ECL intensity, especially without using any coreactant and coreaction accelerator. This resulting ECL bioassay displays excellent performance for ATZ determination with two linear ranges, 0.0001–0.01 μg L−1 as well as 0.01–20 μg L−1, and a detection limit of 0.08 ng L−1(S/N = 3). It exhibits good sensitivity, stability and excellent applicability for the determination of ATZ in real samples. This protocol doesn't require sophisticated instruments and high detection expenses, making it promising to be a reliable alternative in resource–constrained regions. We believe that such newly constructed dual-function S, N-CDs-based ECL bioassay establishes a new way for the enhancement of ECL signal and a versatile strategy for detection of trace analytes.
The authors declare that there is no interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Science Foundation of China (Grant 21475050, 21777052), National Key R&D Program of China (Grant 2018YFC1802003), Self-determined Research Funds of CCNU from the Colleges' Basic Research and Operation of MOE (CCNU18TS013), and The Program of Introducing Talents of Discipline to Universities of China (111 program, B17019).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2019.04.068.
